Per-backbone exact values for all five metrics (FAD-CLAP, CLAP score, FAD-MERT, MAD, Reward) at everyNNare in Table17.

header: Appendix ACalibration: Reliability Diagram and Bins

images.caption: Figure 4:TuneJury calibration onn=2,035n{=}2{,}035held-out test pairs (ties excluded).(a)Reliability diagram: predicted confidence tracks win rate alongy=xy{=}x(pairwise accuracy0.70860.7086, ECE0.03390.0339).(b)Win rate vs. predicted marginm=|s​(A)−s​(B)|m{=}|s(A){-}s(B)|, rising from∼0.46{\sim}0.46atm≤0.13m{\leq}0.13to∼0.97{\sim}0.97atm≥2.64m{\geq}2.64.

Figure 5:OOD recovery on the post-cutoff Music Arena slice. Shaded bands:95%95\%CI. Dashed reference: released TuneJury’s in-distribution agreement on CMI-RewardBench MA (0.720.72).(a)Feb–Mar50/5050/50split (ntest=299n_{\text{test}}{=}299, five seeds): anchor atK=10K{=}10matches retraining atK=250K{=}250(∼25×{\sim}25\timesdata-efficiency edge over the sweptKK-grid), anchor atK=30K{=}30exceeds retraining’s best swept value.(b)Cross-month: Feb–Marβs\beta_{s}fits on April 2026 (ntest=397n_{\text{test}}{=}397, ten seeds), with a within-April sanity probe overlaid. The released checkpoint already reaches∼64%{\sim}64\%raw on April. Feb–Mar fits do not transfer (per-system swings span−10-10to+19+19pp, net cancels).

Figure 6:Decomposition probe (AppendixE, body summary in Section3).(a)Data-scaling curve over the alignment-labeled probe set (PAM++MusicEval, the per-axis MOS pool used only by the probe and distinct from TuneJury’s∼17.5{\sim}17.5K training pairs). Alignment SRCC (blue) and partial SRCC controlling for musicality (red) both rise monotonically within the available range (n=36n{=}36ton=728n{=}728) with no plateau visible at the upper limit, though the probe does not characterize what happens at10×10{\times}or100×100{\times}scale.(b)Multi-seed distribution at full data (n=728n{=}728train,2020-seed). Both partial (95%95\%CI[0.271,0.340][0.271,0.340]) and residual (95%95\%CI[0.417,0.472][0.417,0.472]) intervals exclude zero.

Figure 7:Per-system TuneJury reward vs. win rate on bench-clean held-out splits. Marker shape: circle==open-weights, triangle==proprietary, square==real audio. Color: orange==vocal-capable, blue==instrumental-only, gray==real audio. AIME (ρ=+0.98\rho{=}{+}0.98,n=13n{=}13) and MusicPrefs (ρ=+0.96\rho{=}{+}0.96,n=7n{=}7) show high system-rank agreement between TuneJury and human votes at this small system count. Music Arena (n=74n{=}74) is too small for a reliable per-system signal.

header: Appendix FPer-System Reward Ranking on Held-Out Test Splits

images.caption:
Figure 8:Three population-level probes of the TuneJury reward signal (Section6).Left: lyrics-presence text-proxy probe. Music Arena clips grouped by whether the source pair’slyricsfield was non-empty (a prompt-level proxy for
vocal-request intent, not a per-clip vocal annotation),
with a gap of+0.441{+}0.441reward units atn=6,120n{=}6{,}120. Consistent with a system-level preference confound rather than vocal-quality reward.Middle: external generalization to singing-voice MOS at the system-aggregation level on SingMOS-Pro[60](n=141n{=}141Chinese/Japanese vocal-generation systems), SRCC=+0.44{=}\,{+}0.44. Not direct evidence of vocal-specific features; could reflect general production-quality preference.Right: popularity probe. FMA-Large by listens decile (bottom decile−1.413{-}1.413to
top+0.084{+}0.084,n=106,401n{=}106{,}401). All three probes are population-level.
Per-clip and vocal-specific feature claims are not supported.

images.caption: Figure 9:TuneJury reward distribution across the seven release datasets (extreme tails clipped at[−4,+3.5][-4,+3.5]). All sources are human-music collections except MidiCaps (symbolic MIDI rendered via FluidSynth FluidR3_GM).
